narrative:
First Warn Weather Forecast & Severe Weather Outlook
Tuesday, June 16, 2026
FIRST WARN WEATHER ALERT DAY: WEDNESDAY
Key Messages

A significant severe weather event is possible across portions of Missouri and Illinois on Wednesday as a powerful storm system moves through the Midwest. Two rounds of thunderstorms are expected, with the second round during the afternoon and evening carrying the greatest potential for severe weather.

Damaging straight-line winds appear to be the primary hazard, although large hail, tornadoes, including the possibility of a strong tornado, and localized flash flooding are all concerns. The highest threat currently extends across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois, though all of eastern Missouri and western Illinois should remain weather aware.

In addition to thunderstorms, strong southwest winds may gust between 40 and 45 mph Wednesday afternoon, even outside of storms.

Tuesday Forecast

Today will serve as the calm before a potentially active weather day. A cold front moving through the region will bring scattered showers and a few thunderstorms at times, but widespread severe weather is not expected.

Clouds will mix with periods of sunshine throughout the day. High temperatures will range from the middle 70s across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois to the lower 80s farther south. A few stronger thunderstorms could produce gusty winds this afternoon, but the overall severe weather threat remains low.

By tonight, the front will stall near southeast Missouri before beginning to lift northward as a warm front overnight. This setup will help prepare the atmosphere for a much more volatile weather pattern on Wednesday.

Wednesday Severe Weather Outlook

Wednesday has the potential to become one of the more significant severe weather days of the season across portions of the Midwest.

A strengthening area of low pressure moving through the Upper Midwest will pull warm, humid air northward into Missouri and Illinois while an approaching cold front provides the focus for thunderstorm development. At the same time, exceptionally strong winds throughout the atmosphere will create a favorable environment for organized severe thunderstorms.

Round One: Morning Thunderstorms

The first round of thunderstorms is expected to develop during the morning hours as a powerful low-level jet transports moisture and instability into the region.

These storms will likely develop across Iowa and northern Illinois before moving southeast. The highest threat area during the morning will be across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois. Some of these storms could produce large hail and damaging wind gusts.

Current indications suggest much of the St. Louis metropolitan area may remain south of the most organized morning activity, allowing the atmosphere to recover and destabilize before the main event later in the day.

Round Two: Main Severe Weather Threat

The second round of storms is expected to develop along a cold front between approximately 2 PM and 5 PM Wednesday afternoon.

This round has the greatest potential to produce widespread severe weather.

Temperatures are expected to climb into the upper 80s and lower 90s while dew points rise into the upper 60s and lower 70s. This combination will create a highly unstable atmosphere capable of supporting intense thunderstorms.

Forecast soundings indicate more than enough instability and wind shear to support supercells and organized storm clusters capable of producing all modes of severe weather.

Damaging Wind Threat

Damaging straight-line winds appear to be the greatest overall concern.

Some storms may produce wind gusts between 70 and 80 mph, capable of downing trees, causing widespread power outages, and producing structural damage.

As storms evolve into clusters or lines during the evening hours, the damaging wind threat may become more widespread.

Tornado Threat

Tornadoes are also possible, particularly during the early stages of storm development when individual supercells are more likely.

The greatest tornado threat currently appears to be across west-central Illinois where atmospheric wind shear and storm-relative helicity are forecast to be strongest.

Should isolated supercells remain discrete before storms merge together, a strong tornado cannot be ruled out.

The tornado threat may persist into the evening hours as low-level winds strengthen after sunset.

Large Hail Threat

Large hail will be possible with any supercell thunderstorms that develop during the afternoon.

Hail up to golf ball size or larger may occur, especially across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois where storms have the greatest opportunity to remain isolated during their early stages.

Flash Flooding Threat

As the cold front slows Wednesday night, thunderstorms may repeatedly move across the same locations.

The atmosphere will contain abundant moisture, with rainfall rates potentially exceeding one to two inches per hour in the strongest storms.

Localized flash flooding may develop, especially across portions of central and southern Missouri and Illinois where storms train over the same areas.

Strong Winds Outside of Thunderstorms

Not all of Wednesday's impacts will come from thunderstorms.

A strong low-level jet will create windy conditions throughout the day. Southwest winds may gust between 40 and 45 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon.

A Wind Advisory may eventually be required if confidence continues to increase in these stronger wind gusts reaching the surface.

Areas of Greatest Concern

The highest confidence for significant severe weather currently exists across:

Northeast Missouri
West-Central Illinois
Areas north of Interstate 44 in Missouri
Areas north of Interstate 70 in Illinois

The tornado threat appears greatest across west-central Illinois.

Confidence decreases farther south into southeast Missouri and southern Illinois where storm coverage and intensity remain more uncertain. However, all residents across the First Warn viewing area should closely monitor forecast updates as even minor changes in storm development could shift the corridor of greatest impact.

Wednesday Timeline

Morning (6 AM-Noon)

First round of thunderstorms develops.
Greatest threat across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois.
Large hail and damaging winds possible.

Afternoon (2 PM-5 PM)

Explosive thunderstorm development along the cold front.
Supercells become possible.
All severe weather hazards possible.

Evening (5 PM-10 PM)

Peak severe weather threat.
Damaging winds, tornadoes, and large hail.
Storms become more widespread.

Overnight (10 PM-6 AM)

Severe threat gradually decreases.
Heavy rain and localized flooding become the primary concern.
Thursday Outlook

The cold front will slowly move south and east through the region on Thursday. Additional showers and thunderstorms remain possible, especially south of Interstate 70.

While isolated strong storms cannot be ruled out, the severe weather threat appears significantly lower than Wednesday. Cooler temperatures and lower humidity will begin arriving by late Thursday and continue into Friday.

Bottom Line

Wednesday is shaping up to be a potentially significant severe weather day across portions of Missouri and Illinois. At least some severe thunderstorms are expected, with damaging winds, large hail, tornadoes, and flash flooding all possible.

The greatest threat currently appears to be across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois, though everyone across eastern Missouri and western Illinois should remain weather aware and have multiple ways to receive warnings.

Now is the time to review your severe weather safety plan, ensure weather alerts are enabled on your phone, and be prepared to take action should warnings be issued.

First Warn Weather.
